# Confidential Computing Deep Dive

> A practical guide to protecting data in use with Trusted Execution
> Environments (TEEs), remote attestation, confidential VMs, Kubernetes,
> and CNCF Confidential Containers (CoCo).
> Author: Pradipta Banerjee, Project Maintainer — Confidential Containers

## Chapters

- [Confidential Computing: A Practical Deep Dive](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/intro.md): Learn how confidential computing protects data in use with hardware-based Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs). This practical guide explains remote attestation, AMD SEV-SNP, Intel TDX, confidential
- [The Trust Boundary Problem](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/trust-boundary.md): Running workloads on third-party infrastructure (eg. in public cloud) provides
- [What is Confidential Computing?](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/overview.md): Before getting to confidential computing, we must understand data security challenges and solutions.
- [Confidential Computing Use Cases](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/use-cases.md): Confidential computing is most useful when sensitive data or code must be processed on infrastructure that the workload owner does not fully trust. Common use cases include confidential AI, privacy-pr
- [Confidential Computing Building Blocks](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/building-blocks.md): Confidential computing combines hardware and software building blocks to protect data in use: a Root of Trust, a minimal Trusted Computing Base (TCB), Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs), measured b
- [Confidential Computing Security Concepts](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/concepts.md): The security model behind confidential computing depends on several connected
- [TEE Technologies: AMD SEV-SNP, Intel TDX, and Intel SGX](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/tee-technologies.md): This chapter compares the major Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) technologies — AMD SEV-SNP, Intel TDX, and Intel SGX,
- [Remote Attestation for Confidential Computing](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/remote-attestation.md): expected software stack is running inside a genuine Trusted Execution
- [Known Attacks Against Confidential Computing](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/known-attacks.md): Confidential Computing is not a silver bullet. Researchers have demonstrated attacks that bypass CC protections — particularly those requiring physical access to hardware. Now that the building blocks
- [Three Pillars of Confidential Computing](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/three-pillars.md): Confidential Computing in cloud-native environments is organized into three deployment tiers, each building on the previous one.
- [Confidential Virtual Machine (CVM)](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/confidential-vms.md): A Confidential Virtual Machine (CVM) is a virtual machine that runs inside a hardware Trusted Execution Environment (TEE), protecting the guest's memory and execution from inspection or tampering by t
- [Confidential Containers (CoCo)](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/confidential-containers.md): Confidential Containers protect Kubernetes pods and their data in use by
- [Confidential Kubernetes Clusters](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/confidential-clusters.md): A confidential Kubernetes cluster uses **Confidential Virtual Machines
- [Trustee: Remote Attestation and Secret Management](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/trustee.md): Trustee is the CNCF
- [Confidential Computing for GPUs and Devices](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/gpu-devices.md): The use cases that drive Confidential Computing adoption today, such as model training, inference, and confidential AI services, run on GPUs. But everything covered so far protects CPU memory: a CVM w
- [Confidential Computing Hands-On Labs](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/labs.md): These hands-on confidential computing labs show how to deploy and attest
- [Lab 1: CVM Attestation on Azure](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/lab1-cvm-attestation.md): This lab demonstrates the core attestation loop on a real Confidential VM. You will provision two Azure VMs — a standard Ubuntu VM hosting Trustee (KBS) and a hardware-backed Confidential VM running t
- [Lab 2: CoCo Without Confidential Hardware](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/lab2-coco-without-hw.md): This lab runs the full Confidential Containers stack on any Linux VM — no TEE required. A sample verifier replaces hardware attestation, letting you exercise VM isolation, guest-pull images, and secre
- [Lab 3: CoCo-fy a Workload with cococtl](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/lab3-cococtl.md): `cococtl` (`kubectl coco`) automates the steps from Lab 2: it sets the runtime class, converts Kubernetes Secrets to sealed secrets, generates the initdata annotation (which configures the KBS URL and
- [Lab 4: CoCo on a Real CVM via Peer-Pods (BYOM)](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/lab4-byom.md): This lab runs a Kubernetes cluster on a standard VM and connects it to an Azure Confidential VM as a peer pod. Workload containers execute inside the hardware-backed SEV-SNP CVM, not on the K8s node.
- [Confidential Computing Resources and References](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/chapters/references.md): Use this curated collection of confidential computing standards, research

## Full book (single file)

- [llms-full.txt](https://cc-book.github.io/cc-book/llms-full.txt): All chapters concatenated into one document.
